title: SEGMENT 11 — Phage cocktails: the strategy for broadening coverage

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Phage cocktails: the strategy for broadening coverage

One of the practical answers to the narrow host-range problem is the phage cocktail — combining multiple phages into a single therapeutic preparation, so that the cocktail covers a broader range of bacterial strains and is less likely to be defeated by any single resistance mutation.
The logic is straightforward.
Suppose you have a phage A that kills 60% of clinical Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolates.
That's not great coverage on its own.
But suppose you can pair it with phage B that kills a different, partially overlapping 60%, and phage C that kills yet another partially overlapping 60%.
The union of the three — the fraction of clinical isolates susceptible to at least one of A, B, or C — might be 90% or 95%.
By stacking phages with overlapping but not identical host ranges, you broaden coverage.
And as long as the cocktail contains at least one phage active against any given patient's isolate, the cocktail works.
The cocktail also addresses resistance evolution.
Bacteria evolving resistance to phage A might require one mutation.
Evolving resistance to A and B and C simultaneously requires three mutations — and if the three phages bind to different receptors, those mutations are independent.
The compound probability of simultaneous resistance is the product of the individual probabilities, which is vanishingly small.
So a well-designed cocktail isn't just broader-spectrum, it's also harder for the bacteria to evade.
This is the same logic as combination therapy in HIV and tuberculosis — combine multiple drugs with different mechanisms, and evolution can't escape all of them at once.
The analogy I want you to hold for this segment is: cocktails are the way to broaden coverage.
One phage targets one bacterium.
Mix five phages, cover a broader range — and make resistance much harder.
In practice, designing a cocktail involves a screening process.
You take your phage library — say, the Eliava Institute's collection, or a curated library from a phage company — and you test each phage against a panel of clinical isolates of the target pathogen.
You score each phage for its host range.

You pick a set of three to ten phages that together cover the broadest fraction of isolates, that use distinct surface receptors (so resistance to one doesn't confer resistance to others), and that have compatible production characteristics.
The cocktail is then manufactured as a single product, with each phage component characterized by titer.
The two major approaches to cocktails differ in how customized the cocktail is.

The fixed-cocktail approach, which is what most Western phage companies are pursuing, uses a single standardized product — three or five phages mixed in defined ratios — that's been tested against a large panel of clinical isolates and shown to cover a high fraction.
This is easier to manufacture, easier to regulate, and works for empirical use without per-patient matching.
The downside is that it doesn't cover the resistant fraction.
The personalized-cocktail approach, which is closer to the Eliava tradition, custom-builds a cocktail for each patient.
You isolate the patient's pathogen, test it against the phage library, pick the phages that work best against this specific isolate, and prepare a cocktail just for this patient.
This gives much higher hit rates but is logistically much harder and regulatorily much messier.
Some hybrid approaches — pre-build a few standard cocktails for the most common pathogens, customize when the standard cocktails fail — are emerging.
There's also a useful concept called phage-antibiotic synergy, which is when a phage and an antibiotic together kill better than either alone.

In some documented cases, the phage forces the bacterium to alter its surface to escape phage killing, and that alteration sensitizes the bacterium to an antibiotic it had previously been resistant to.
Or the antibiotic stresses the bacterium in a way that enhances phage replication.
The interactions are complex and case-by-case, but the practical implication is that phage therapy doesn't have to replace antibiotics — it can be combined with them, sometimes synergistically.
